text: The surgical setup includes one 12mm trocar for the assistant and three 8mm robotic trocars for the operating arms of the robot, positioned for the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a suture with a large straight needle at each end to retract the round ligament, facilitating exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon passes large straight needles through the abdominal wall and secures them outside the patient using an instrument, facilitating retraction of anatomical structures.
The third robotic arm is visible performing tasks other than liver retraction, facilitated by the innovative liver retraction method used in patients who have undergone cholecystectomy.
A close-up view shows the suture passed through the skin, secured by a surgical instrument, demonstrating the technique used for liver retraction.
The surgeon performs a cloture maneuver using robotic surgery after the hepatic flexure of the colon has been widely mobilized.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the robotic arm to apply traction near the duodenal wall, ensuring not to perforate the tissue during the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon retracts the first jejunal loop through the mesenteric opening created by duodenal mobilization and transection of Treitz's ligament, all within the supracolic compartment.
The surgeon uses an endoscopic stapler to transect the first jejunal loop, completing the division of this intestinal segment.
The surgeon dissects the superior pancreaticoduodenal artery, carefully isolating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon applies polymer locking systems to secure the suture, ensuring stability and closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon divides the superior pancreaticoduodenal artery between polymer locking systems, ensuring the pulse of the common hepatic artery is intact before proceeding.
The surgeon dissects the main bile duct within the hepatoduodenal ligament, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon transects the main bile duct, completing the separation of this structure from surrounding tissues.
The biliary stent is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the biliary stent from the transected main bile duct, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the distal segment of the main duct, which will be part of the surgical specimen, using a locking system.
A detachable laparoscopic bulldog clamp is applied to the proximal segment of the main duct to prevent leakage during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the sectioning of the main bile duct, finalizing the transection process.
The surgeon ligates and transects the gastroepiploic vessels and pyloric artery, followed by transecting the first part of the duodenum using a surgical stapler.
The surgeon dissects the inferior pancreatic border, carefully exposing the mesenterico-portal confluence, which is the junction of the superior mesenteric vein and the portal vein.
The surgeon develops a tunnel around the superior mesenteric vein and portal confluence, carefully dissecting and exposing these vascular structures.
An instrument is maneuvered in front of the portal confluence and beneath the pancreatic body, indicating the ongoing dissection and separation of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a vascular tape to encircle the pancreas, visually isolating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses vascular tape to retract the pancreas away from the mesenteric vein, beginning the dissection of the pancreatic head and uncinate process from the mesenteric root and vein.
The surgeon dissects the uncinate process and tumor from the mesenteric vein, carefully separating these structures from the vein using precise surgical techniques.
The surgeon carefully secures the mesenteric vein while performing a dissection plane close to the superior mesenteric artery, ensuring precise separation of tissues for oncologic purposes.

The surgeon encounters difficulty dissecting the pancreas from the superior mesenteric vein due to inflammation in the pancreatic head and uncinate process, although the vein is not involved by the tumor.
The surgeon proceeds with the transection of the pancreas, following the dissection of the pancreatic head and uncinate process from the mesenteric vein.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue in the region of the pancreas to identify the pancreatic duct, focusing on the area near the previously dissected uncinate process and mesenteric vein.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect tissue, likely focusing on the area around the pancreatic head and uncinate process, as previously described.
The surgeon transects the main pancreatic duct, completing the separation of the duct from surrounding pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon completes the transection of the pancreatic tissue, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon ligates and transects the inferior pancreatic artery, which is a branch of the superior mesenteric artery, using surgical instruments.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the uncinate process from the mesenteric and superior mesenteric artery, carefully separating the pancreatic tissue from these vascular structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection, releasing the pancreas and the entire surgical specimen from the portal vein, ensuring complete separation of these structures.
At the conclusion of the resection phase, the inferior vena cava, clamped bile duct, and pancreas are visible in the surgical field.

The surgical field displays the pancreatic duct, superior mesenteric vein, portal vein, and superior mesenteric artery, highlighting the anatomical structures involved in the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the reconstruction phase by performing a pancreaticojejunal anastomosis, connecting the pancreas to the jejunum.
The surgeon positions the first jejunal loop through the mesenteric defect created during the resection phase, ensuring proper alignment for subsequent anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a proline suture on the inferior border of the pancreas to retract it into a stable position, assisted by robotic arm III.
The surgeon prepares the cut surface of the pancreatic remnant by mobilizing approximately one centimeter from the splenic vein, creating space for the upcoming anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side duct-to-mucosa anastomosis, connecting the pancreatic duct to the jejunal mucosa.

The surgeon performs the posterior outer layer anastomosis using 4-0 Prolene sutures, connecting the posterior aspect of the pancreas to the seromuscular layer of the jejunum at the anti-mesenteric border.
The surgeon applies a running suture to the outer posterior layer of the anastomosis site, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon opens the jejunum on the anti-mesenteric side using a harmonic scalpel, aligning the incision with the location of the pancreatic duct.
The surgeon performs a duct-to-mucosa anastomosis using interrupted sutures with Vicryl 5-0, carefully attaching the pancreatic duct to the mucosal layer.
The robotic surgical system is actively engaged in the procedure, with one assistant visible in the operative field.
The surgeon passes sutures at the three, five, eight, and eleven o'clock positions, along with two anterior sutures, during the anastomosis procedure.

The surgeon uses Forzero's ProGlean sutures to perform the anterior outer layer anastomosis in an interrupted fashion, connecting the anterior aspect of the pancreatic parenchyma with the jejunal seromuscular layer.
The completed pancreatic jejunal anastomosis is visible, showcasing the precise suturing facilitated by the robotic system.
The surgeon begins the baleodigestive anastomosis, utilizing the robotic system to enhance precision and control during the procedure.
The surgeon removes the bulldog clamp from the main bile duct, allowing for the restoration of bile flow following the anastomosis.
The surgeon creates an opening on the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum, located approximately twenty centimeters from the previously completed pancreatic jejunal anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the reconstruction in a single-loop manner, visually integrating the pancreatic and jejunal structures.
The surgeon performs the baleodigestive anastomosis using two running sutures, one placed posteriorly and the other anteriorly, with a 5-0 Fico suture.

The liver is retracted using a transcutaneous suture applied to the round ligament, while the third robotic arm is utilized to expose and stabilize the bile duct in a favorable position for anastomosis.
The completed baleodigestive anastomosis is visible, showing the connection between the bile duct and the digestive tract.
The final aspect of the baleodigestive anastomosis is shown, with the completed sutures securing the bile duct in place, demonstrating the successful reconstruction in a single-loop manner.
The surgeon fixes the jejunal loop, used for the terrenes mesocolic anastomosis, to the mesocolon to prevent herniation of the jejunum through the mesentery.
In the inframesocolic compartment, the surgeon identifies the jejunal loop emerging from the mesocolon opening through the Trots ligament.
The surgeon positions the jejunal loop to perform a pre-colic duodenum jejunal anastomosis, preparing the anatomical structures for the connection between the duodenum and jejunum.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis in an end-to-side fashion, connecting the jejunal loop to the duodenum.

The surgeon performs a running suture using PDS 4-0 between the seromuscular layers of the duodenum and the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum, completing the posterior outer layer of the anastomosis.
The surgeon opens the posterior layer of the duodenum near the stapled tissue, revealing the internal structures of the duodenum.
The surgeon creates an opening on the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum, corresponding to the duodenal stump, using a harmonic scalpel. The mucosa is fixed onto the serosa of the jejunum.
The surgeon performs an inner-posterior continuous absorbable suture using PDS 4-0, ensuring all layers of the duodenum and jejunum are securely stitched together.
The surgeon completes the transection of the stapled portion of the duodenum, separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses robotic arm number three to stabilize the anastomosis between the duodenum and jejunum, ensuring the connection remains secure during the procedure.
The surgeon performs the inner anterior layer of the anastomosis, completing the connection between the duodenal stump and the jejunum.
The surgeon completes the duodenal jejunum anastomosis using an anterior outer interrupted suture with PDS 4-0, ensuring all tissue layers of both the duodenum and jejunum are securely joined.
The completion of the duodenum jejunal anastomosis is visually confirmed, showing the final sutured connection between the duodenum and jejunum.
The completion of the reconstructive phase of the pancreatic duodenectomy is visually evident, with all anastomoses stabilized and the transected duodenum portion secured.
The surgeon removes the transcutaneous suture that was previously used to retract the liver, allowing the liver to return to its natural position.
The surgeon places a passive open system drain near the pancreatic jejunal anastomosis, ensuring proper drainage from the surgical site.

The surgical specimen is retracted inside a plastic bag through an enlarged twelve millimeter trocar located inferior to the umbilicus in the midline, which was used for the robotic camera during the procedure.
A 19 French blade drain is positioned near the bileodigestive anastomosis following specimen retrieval.
The surgeon sutures the incisions, marking the completion of the robotic pancreatic duodenectomy procedure.
